cue: How do you say everything's going great?

es.literal: to go all wind at the stern
es.meaning: For everything to be going smoothly and successfully.
es.example: Desde el ascenso, todo le va viento en popa.
es.exampleTranslation: Since the promotion, everything's going his way.

it.meaning: For everything to be going wonderfully.
it.example: Dopo la promozione, gli va tutto a gonfie vele.
it.exampleTranslation: Since the promotion, it's all smooth sailing.

bridge.note: Both languages put success on a boat under good wind, and each looks at a different part of the rig. Spanish feels the WIND at the stern — viento en popa, the breeze pushing from behind, the invisible force doing the work. Italian sees the SAILS themselves — a gonfie vele, swollen full, the visible result of that same wind. Cause versus effect, on the same happy voyage.

interference.es_to_it: 'Vento in poppa' does exist in Italian, but the more idiomatic phrase looks at the sails: a gonfie vele.
interference.it_to_es: 'A velas hinchadas' is not the Spanish idiom; Spanish feels the wind at the stern — viento en popa.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Wind-vs-sails, cause-vs-effect. Note IT also has vento in poppa as a near-calque — reviewer to rank.
